events.label: Pentecost
events.desc: The Holy Spirit descends on the disciples in Jerusalem; 3,000 are baptised — the church is born.

events.label: Stephen Martyred
events.desc: The first Christian martyr is stoned in Jerusalem, triggering a persecution that scatters believers across Judea and Samaria.

events.label: Paul's Conversion
events.desc: Saul of Tarsus, the church's chief persecutor, is encountered by the risen Christ on the road to Damascus and becomes its greatest missionary.

events.label: Jerusalem Council
events.desc: Apostles and elders gather in Jerusalem to settle whether Gentile converts must be circumcised — establishing grace apart from the Mosaic law.

events.label: Jerusalem Destroyed
events.desc: Roman general Titus destroys the Temple, fulfilling Christ's prophecy. The church, already scattered, becomes irreversibly Gentile-majority.

events.label: Apostolic Witness Closes
events.desc: With John's death on Patmos, the last eyewitness to Christ departs. The Apostolic Age ends; the church now lives by the completed written testimony.

events.label: Polycarp Martyred
events.desc: Bishop of Smyrna and disciple of the apostle John, Polycarp is burned alive at age 86. His death epitomises the ante-Nicene church's theology of martyrdom.

events.label: Justin Martyr
events.desc: The philosopher-apologist defends Christianity before Roman emperors, pioneering Christian engagement with Greco-Roman thought before his own martyrdom.

events.label: Irenaeus — Against Heresies
events.desc: Bishop of Lyon refutes Gnosticism with a systematic defence of apostolic tradition, Scripture, and episcopal succession — the first great work of Christian theology.

events.label: Tertullian: Latin Theology
events.desc: Carthaginian lawyer-theologian forges Latin Christian vocabulary — including trinitas (Trinity) — and becomes the father of Western theology.

events.label: Origen — Alexandrian School
events.desc: Origen of Alexandria produces the Hexapla, systematic theology (De Principiis), and a prolific biblical commentary tradition — the most productive scholar of the early church.

events.label: Diocletian Persecution
events.desc: The Great Persecution: Diocletian orders churches burned, Scriptures destroyed, and clergy imprisoned. The church's refusal to surrender its Scriptures — the traditores — shapes canon debates for generations.

events.label: Council of Nicaea I
events.desc: Constantine gathers 318 bishops at Nicaea to settle the Arian controversy. The council affirms Christ is homoousios — of the same substance as the Father.

events.label: Athanasius Contra Mundum
events.desc: Bishop of Alexandria is exiled five times for defending Nicene orthodoxy against Arian emperors. His tenacity earns the phrase 'Athanasius against the world.'

events.label: Council of Constantinople I
events.desc: Expanded the Nicene Creed, affirming the full divinity of the Holy Spirit (against Macedonians) and settling the final shape of Trinitarian doctrine.

events.label: Jerome — The Vulgate
events.desc: Jerome translates the Hebrew Old Testament and Greek New Testament into Latin — the Vulgate becomes the Bible of Western Christendom for a thousand years.

events.label: Augustine of Hippo
events.desc: Bishop of Hippo produces the Confessions, City of God, and definitive treatises on grace, free will, and original sin — the most influential theologian in Western history.

events.label: Council of Chalcedon
events.desc: The fourth ecumenical council defines Christ as one Person in two natures — fully divine and fully human — using the four adverbs: inconfusedly, unchangeably, indivisibly, inseparably.

events.label: Council of Orange II
events.desc: Caesarius of Arles leads a provincial synod in southern Gaul that condemns Semi-Pelagianism, affirming that even the first movement of the will toward God is a gift of grace.

events.label: Rule of Saint Benedict
events.desc: Benedict of Nursia writes his Rule organising monastic life around prayer, work, and hospitality. Benedictine monasteries become the centres of literacy, mission, and agriculture in medieval Europe.

events.label: Gregory the Great
events.desc: Pope Gregory I sends Augustine of Canterbury to evangelise England, reforms the liturgy, and codifies pastoral theology — defining the medieval papal office.

events.label: Rise of Islam
events.desc: Muhammad's death launches rapid Islamic expansion. By 732 Muslim forces reach Tours, France. North Africa, the Middle East, and Persia — once heartlands of the church — are largely lost.

events.label: Charlemagne Crowned
events.desc: Pope Leo III crowns Charlemagne Holy Roman Emperor — cementing the fusion of church and state that defines medieval Christendom and sets the stage for centuries of church-empire conflict.

events.label: The Filioque Dispute
events.desc: Western churches insert filioque ('and the Son') into the Nicene Creed without an ecumenical council. Eastern patriarch Photius condemns it — the first major clash before the Great Schism.

events.label: The Great Schism
events.desc: Cardinal Humbert and Patriarch Cerularius exchange mutual excommunications. Rome and Constantinople divide into Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox, a separation unhealed to this day.

events.label: Anselm — Cur Deus Homo
events.desc: Archbishop of Canterbury writes the classic formulation of the satisfaction theory of atonement — why the God-man must die to satisfy the honour (and later, justice) of God.

events.label: The Crusades Begin
events.desc: Pope Urban II launches the First Crusade to reclaim Jerusalem. The crusading movement spans two centuries, leaving a lasting wound between Christianity, Islam, and Eastern Orthodoxy.

events.label: Thomas Aquinas
events.desc: Dominican theologian synthesises Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology in the Summa Theologica — the summit of Scholasticism and the foundation of Roman Catholic systematic theology.

events.label: John Wycliffe
events.desc: Oxford theologian attacks papal corruption and translates the Bible into English — the 'Morning Star of the Reformation.' His followers, the Lollards, precede Luther by 150 years.

events.label: Jan Hus Burned
events.desc: Czech reformer Jan Hus is burned at the Council of Constance despite a promise of safe conduct. His martyrdom ignites the Hussite movement and plants the seed of the Reformation in Bohemia.

events.label: Gutenberg Bible
events.desc: Johannes Gutenberg prints the first Bible with movable type. Mass printing transforms Scripture access, making the coming Reformation theologically explosive in a way prior movements could not be.

events.label: Luther's 95 Theses
events.desc: Martin Luther posts his 95 Theses in Wittenberg, igniting the Protestant Reformation with the doctrine of justification by faith alone (sola fide) through grace alone (sola gratia).

events.label: Diet of Worms
events.desc: Luther refuses to recant before Emperor Charles V: 'Here I stand.' The moment seals the break with Rome and the principle that Scripture alone (sola Scriptura) is the supreme authority.

events.label: Anabaptist Movement
events.desc: The Radical Reformation begins in Zurich. Anabaptists reject infant baptism and state-church fusion, practising believer's baptism and voluntary church membership — forerunners of Baptist and free-church traditions.

events.label: Calvin's Geneva
events.desc: John Calvin establishes Geneva as a model Reformed city and produces the Institutes of the Christian Religion — the definitive systematic theology of the Reformed tradition and a blueprint for Reformed polity worldwide.

events.label: Council of Trent
events.desc: The Roman Catholic Counter-Reformation response to Protestantism: Scripture plus tradition as co-equal authority, justification by infused grace, and the seven sacraments reaffirmed. The confessional divide solidifies.

events.label: Westminster Assembly
events.desc: English and Scottish divines produce the Westminster Confession of Faith and Catechisms — the most precise and enduring summary of Reformed theology, still confessed by Presbyterians worldwide.

events.label: The Great Awakening
events.desc: Jonathan Edwards and George Whitefield lead a transatlantic revival. The Awakening reshapes American religion, stresses conversion, and contributes to the evangelical tradition and eventually the abolition movement.

events.label: Wesley and Methodism
events.desc: John Wesley's Aldersgate experience and open-air preaching births Methodism — emphasising personal holiness, free grace, and discipleship classes that transformed working-class England and seeded global Wesleyan Christianity.

events.label: William Carey — Modern Missions
events.desc: Baptist cobbler-theologian sails to India and founds the modern Protestant missionary movement, translating Scripture into dozens of Indian languages and inspiring a century of global missions.

events.label: C.H. Spurgeon
events.desc: The Prince of Preachers preaches to thousands weekly at the Metropolitan Tabernacle, produces thousands of sermons still in print, and establishes Spurgeon's College — the most influential Baptist preacher in history.

events.label: Azusa Street Revival
events.desc: William Seymour leads a three-year revival in Los Angeles that launches the global Pentecostal movement — now the fastest-growing segment of world Christianity, numbering over 600 million.

events.label: Fundamentalist Controversy
events.desc: The Fundamentalist-Modernist controversy divides American Protestantism. Publication of The Fundamentals and J. Gresham Machen's Christianity and Liberalism define the fault lines of 20th-century theological debate.

events.label: Billy Graham Crusades
events.desc: Billy Graham preaches to over 200 million people across six decades, becoming the world's most recognisable evangelist and the face of postwar neo-evangelical Protestantism.

events.label: Vatican II
events.desc: The Second Vatican Council, called by Pope John XXIII, opens the Roman Catholic Church to liturgical reform, ecumenical dialogue, and engagement with modernity — the most significant Catholic event since Trent.

events.label: Global Christianity
events.desc: The centre of world Christianity shifts to Africa, Asia, and Latin America. The Global South now holds a majority of the world's Christians — a demographic transformation unmatched since the first century.
